data.title: Business Inventories Rose in February
data.description:
By Matt Grossman

U.S. businesses increased in February, the Commerce Department said Tuesday.

Inventories stood at $2.687 trillion in February, up 0.4% from January. Analysts polled by The Wall Street Journal were expecting a 0.3% increase.

Distributive trade sales and manufacturers' shipments climbed by 1.7% in February to $2.015 trillion.

The inventory-to-sales ratio was 1.33, down from 1.39 12 months earlier.

Write to Matt Grossman at matt.grossman@wsj.com

data.title: Nio stock just flagged a mega bullish pattern pointing to a 70% surge
data.description:
Nio stock price has pulled back in the past two days as investors booked profits after the recent surge that pushed it from a low of $4.3 in February to a high of $7. This retreat may be a good entry point as the stock has formed a highly bullish chart pattern.

Nio stock price is forming a cup and handle pattern 

The daily chart shows that the Nio share price bottomed at $4.37 in February as investors remained concerned about its dilution.

It then started a slow comeback that accelerated after it released its financial results, which showed that it made a profit in the fourth quarter.

A closer look at this chart shows that the stock formed a golden cross as the 50-day and 200-day Exponential Moving Averages (EMA) crossed each other. A golden cross normally confirms a bullish breakout as it sends a signal that the short-term momentum is gaining steam.

Most importantly, the stock is slowly forming a cup-and-handle pattern whose upper side is at $8, and the lower side is at $4.36. The profit target in a C&H pattern is estimated by subtracting the lower side from the upper side. In this case, the difference is $3.65.

Adding this amount to the cup’s upper side brings the target to $11.65. A surge to that level is 70% from the current level. On the flip side, a move below the key support level at $5 will invalidate the bullish outlook.

Nio stock chart | Source: TradingView

Nio’s business is firing on all cylinders 

Fundamentally, Nio’s business is tiring on all cylinders, even as the EV business faces major headwinds and some of the top companies slow.

Tesla, the most popular EV company, recently published weak delivery numbers, with the gap between production and deliveries soaring to 50k.

Similarly, BYD, the biggest EV company in China, also reported weak numbers, confirming that its business was slowing. Other top EV companies like Li Auto and XPeng are also struggling.

On the other hand, Nio’s vehicle deliveries have surged in the past few months. Its recent numbers showed that its vehicle deliveries soared by 136% to 34,486 in March, bringing the quarterly figure to 83,465. The company has now delivered over 1.081 million since its inception.

The company’s revenue jumped by 75% in the fourth quarter to over $4.95 billion, making it one of the fastest-growing companies in the industry. Its gross profit rose by 163%, while the net profit rose to $40 million. It was the first time that the company published a quarter profit.

Nio also ended the last quarter with a good balance sheet with over $6.6 billion in cash and short-term investments. This growth is expected to accelerate after the recent launch of the ES9 vehicle. Its ONVO L90 has also continued doing well, becoming the best-selling large BEV SUV last year.

Nio’s management believes that the company has more room to grow this year, helped by its investments across multiple brands and the potential for international growth.

For example, the company may target the Canadian market, where tariffs for Chinese EVs dropped from 100% to 6%. It is also aiming to gain share in other countries, including in Europe, Latin America, and Southeast Asia.

data.title: Netherlands ends nationwide order to keep chickens inside due to bird flu
data.description:
The Dutch government on Tuesday said it would end a nationwide order to keep chickens inside to contain the spread of bird flu, as the risk of contagion had fallen.

The order was issued in October last year as infections spread rapidly on farms across the Netherlands, a major exporter of poultry, eggs and other agricultural products.

The government said the situation would be closely monitored as bird flu had not yet disappeared. It maintained the order to keep chickens inside in a part of the Gelderland province where a relatively large number of poultry farms are located.

data.title: Trump to United and American: Don't Merge — WSJ
data.description:
By Alison Sider

President Trump threw cold water on the idea of a merger between United Airlines and American Airlines, adding to Washington lawmakers' dim view of a potential megadeal.

"I don't like having them merge," he told CNBC on Tuesday. Both airlines are doing well, he said.

He drew a comparison to consolidation in the aerospace and defense industry, which he said has left the U.S. with fewer players. "It makes them lazy," Trump said.

American shares fell 1% Tuesday morning, while United rose 1.5%.

Trump's comments came after reports last week that United Chief Executive Scott Kirby had pitched President Trump on a potential megadeal.

The suggestion of bringing together two of the largest U.S. airlines has already sparked blowback from lawmakers on both sides of the aisle and consumer groups. Industry officials have said combining United and American would raise significant antitrust concerns.

American has publicly blasted the idea as anticompetitive and has said it has no interest. United declined to comment Tuesday.

Write to Alison Sider at alison.sider@wsj.com

data.title: US dollar gains on Iran peace optimism and upbeat retail sales
data.description:
Key points:
  • Ceasefire hopes rise as US, Iran consider talks in Pakistan, analysts say
  • US retail sales beat forecasts in March, boosted by higher gasoline prices
  • Traders eye Kevin Warsh's Senate testimony for Fed policy direction

By Karen Brettell

The U.S. dollar edged higher on Tuesday on optimism that the U.S. will reach a ceasefire deal in the war with Iran, while retail sales data for March pointed to a strong U.S. economy.

The U.S. military said on Tuesday it had seized a tanker linked to Iran in international waters, its latest apparent action to enforce a blockade, with time running out on a ceasefire and the prospect of last-ditch further peace talks still up in the air.

Traders remain positive that a deal will be reached with U.S. Vice President JD Vance expected in Islamabad on Wednesday to hold talks to end the war .

“The market is taking a great deal of solace that both sides are travelling to Pakistan,” said Adam Button, chief currency analyst at investingLive.

The dollar also briefly bounced after U.S. retail sales increased more than expected in March as the war with Iran boosted gasoline prices and led to a record surge in receipts at service stations, while tax refunds underpinned spending elsewhere.

“The data in the U.S. is telling a consistent story of a decent acceleration,” said Button. “That's been obscured by the war and it should be a U.S. dollar tailwind because I find it hard to believe that we will go back to pricing in two cuts this year.”

Fed fund futures traders are currently pricing in only 36% odds of one 25-basis point cut this year.

Traders are also focused on Kevin Warsh’s testimony to the U.S. Senate on Tuesday for signs of how he would guide monetary policy if he is appointed Federal Reserve Chair.

Warsh, U.S. President Donald Trump's nominee to lead the U.S. central bank, will tell lawmakers that he is "committed to ensuring that the conduct of monetary policy remains strictly independent ," according to prepared remarks released on Monday.

Some traders and analysts, however, anticipate that Warsh may be less dovish than his comments indicate if he is appointed to the role.

The dollar index , which measures the greenback against a basket of currencies including the yen and the euro, was last up 0.1% at 98.17, with the euro down 0.16% at $1.1768.

Against the Japanese yen , the dollar strengthened 0.19% to 159.09.

data.title: Wall Street indexes gain even with ceasefire in question
data.description:
Key points:
  • U.S. indexes up modestly, Dow leads with ~0.5% advance
  • Cons Disc leads S&P 500 sector gainers; Real Estate falls most
  • Euro STOXX 600 index slips ~0.4%
  • Dollar up; U.S. crude gains >1%; bitcoin slips; gold off >1%
  • U.S. 10-year Treasury yield rises to ~4.28%

US EQUITY INDEXES GAIN EVEN WITH CEASEFIRE IN QUESTION

Wall Street's three major indexes are rising on Tuesday with boosts from Amazon.com and UnitedHealth while investors appear to be looking through uncertainty around the prospects for a U.S.-Iran peace process as the end of a two-week ceasefire period nears.

A senior Iranian official told Reuters on Monday that Iran is considering attending peace talks with the United States in Pakistan while U.S. President Donald Trump told CNBC on Tuesday that he did not want to extend the ceasefire, as the U.S. was in a strong negotiating position and would end up with what he called a great deal. Washington has also expressed confidence that talks with Iran will go ahead in Pakistan.

Providing the biggest boost to the S&P 500 is Amazon.com AMZN.O after it said late on Monday that it will invest up to $25 billion in Anthropic, signaling Big Tech is still willing to pour money into AI. Amazon shares rose 2.7%. On the earnings front, UnitedHealth UNH.N shares are jumping 8.8% after the health insurer raised its annual profit forecast and beat Wall Street expectations for the first quarter.

Wall Street analysts said the focus will be on whether Ternus can sustain growth by fusing the company's hardware heritage with artificial intelligence, an area where Apple has lagged.

Meanwhile, the next leadership of the Federal Reserve is also on investors' minds as former governor Kevin Warsh takes the next step to becoming the head of the U.S. central bank with a hearing before the Senate Banking Committee. While his confirmation is ultimately expected to go ahead, it will be against the backdrop of a dispute over a Trump administration criminal probe of current Fed Chair Jerome Powell.

Among the S&P 500's 11 major industry sectors, five are advancing. Consumer Discretionary is the strongest, up nearly 1%. Defensive Utilities is the weakest, down 0.7%.

Here is your morning snapshot from 10:20 ET (1420 GMT):

data.title: United Airlines Earnings Land Next. Merger Talk, Fuel Costs, Airfares, and More to Watch. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Callum Keown

United Airlines investors saw one flight path to growth closed off as American Airlines categorically snubbed its merger pitch last week. The carrier's earnings now need to show it can keep growing in other ways.

The stock has been in recovery mode, up more than 7% this month, but remains at the whim of volatile oil markets and elevated jet fuel prices amid the Iran war. The shares are down around 12% in 2026, lagging behind rival Delta Air Lines, up 2.6% and Southwest Airlines, 1.2% higher. United stock rose 1.3% in early trading.

CEO Scott Kirby is likely to face questions over his pitch to merge with American Airlines, which he reportedly made to senior government officials just days before the Iran war.

President Donald Trump isn't a fan of the idea. "I don't like having them merge," he said on CNBC. And since American doesn't like it either, it looks the deal won't get off the ground.

Fuel costs will be the sticking point. Alaska Air suspended its full-year guidance and posted a wider-than-expected first-quarter loss late Monday due to rising fuel expenses.

"Fuel remains the largest source of near-term uncertainty," the company said. They expect fuel prices to average $4.50 a gallon in the second quarter, up from $2.98 in the first quarter. That adds around $600 million in costs in the current quarter, or an earnings-per-share headwind of $3.60, it added.

Delta fared better earlier this month, and the carrier's earnings set the benchmark for United and others. Despite a more than $2 billion fuel cost increase, the airline said it expects to post a $1 billion pretax profit in the second quarter.

Demand is strong and consumers aren't balking at higher fares, plus it's cutting capacity to mitigate the impact of higher fuel prices. It also owns its own refinery, which is set to benefit Delta to the tune of $300 million.

United doesn't have a refinery, but strong demand and airfare hikes can help it, too.

The carrier posts its numbers after the bell today and analysts are expecting earnings of $1.08 per share on revenue of $14.4 billion, according to a FactSet poll of estimates. For the second quarter — typically a strong period for airlines as travelers book and take summer vacations — Wall Street is looking for EPS of $2.15 on revenue of $1.96 billion.

"Delta's strong demand commentary and low teens second-quarter revenue guidance has laid out a market through which other airlines are likely to be judged this week," UBS analyst Atul Maheswari said in a note last week.

The bar for United may be even higher, Maheswari added, noting that he was expecting second-quarter revenue guidance of 14.8% growth. United should see faster revenue growth than Delta given the larger impact disruption at Newark Airport had on the carrier in the comparable period, he added.

Full-year guidance will also be in focus, particularly as Delta and Alaska didn't offer any. United took the unusual step last year of issuing two guidance scenarios in the wake of President Donald Trump's sweeping global tariffs.

It could do something similar again.

Write to Callum Keown at callum.keown@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Goodfood Shares Sink After Regulatory Suspension Hits Sales, User Base
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

Goodfood Market shares fell sharply Tuesday after the company reported a wider loss in the second quarter as declining users and a regulatory shutdown dragged on sales.

Shares traded 23% lower at 17 Canadian cents (12 U.S. cents).

The Canadian online grocery and meal-solutions company said a regulatory suspension of its license in the quarter was one of the main drags.

The Canadian Food Inspection Agency in December suspended the Safe Food for Canadians license of the company in Saint-Laurent, Quebec, prohibiting the company from conducting any activities through the license. The license was reinstated on Jan. 8.

Goodfood said the result of the suspension was a decline in users, which added to the pressure in the quarter.

Second-quarter sales fell 26% to C$22.5 million, while loss widened to C$6.77 million, or C$0.07 a share, from a loss of C$2.39 million, or C$0.03 a share, a year earlier.

"Second quarter results reflect the impact of a temporary disruption and softer demand. More importantly, CFIA clarified what needed to change--and we are acting on it decisively," said Chairman and Chief Executive Selim Bassoul.

Bassoul said that he and the president of the company, Najib Maalouf, have decided to forego their base salaries in response to the current circumstances.

"We believe that in this phase of the Company's transformation, accountability needs to start at the top," Bassoul said.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com

data.title: Valmont Industries Inc reports results for the quarter ended March 31 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• Valmont Industries Inc reported quarterly adjusted earnings of $5.51​​ per share for the quarter ended March 31, higher than the same quarter last year, when the company reported EPS of $4.32. The mean expectation of five analysts for the quarter was for earnings of $4.73 per share. Wall Street expected results to range from $4.52 to $4.99 per share.

  • Revenue rose 6.2% to $1.03 billion from a year ago; analysts expected $995.88 million.

  • Valmont Industries Inc's reported EPS for the quarter was $5.51​.

  • The company reported quarterly net income of $108.03 million.

  • Valmont Industries Inc shares had risen by 2.6% this quarter and gained 1.9% so far this year.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had fallen by about 1.5% in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, there have been no negative revisions of earnings estimates

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 5 "strong buy" or "buy," 1 "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell." The average consensus recommendation for the construction & engineering peer group is also "buy"

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Valmont Industries Inc is $490.44, about 16.4% above its last closing price of $409.88
